records.ety: [OF. guarder, garder, warder, F. garder, fr. OHG. wart<?/n to be on the watch, await, G. marten. See Ward, v. & n., and cf. Guard, n.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Guarded; p. pr. & vb. n. Gurding.]

defs: To protect from danger; to secure against surprise, attack, or injury; to keep in safety; to defend; to shelter; to shield from surprise or attack; to protect by attendance; to accompany for protection; to care for.
quotes: For Heaven still guards the right.

defs: To keep watch over, in order to prevent escape or restrain from acts of violence, or the like.
To protect the edge of, esp. with an ornamental border; hence, to face or ornament with lists, laces, etc.
quotes: The body of your discourse is sometime guarded with fragments, and the guards are but slightly basted on neither.

defs: To fasten by binding; to gird.
To watch by way of caution or defense; to be cautious; to be in a state or position of defense or safety; as, careful persons guard against mistakes.

records.ety: [OF. guarde, F. garde; of German origin; cf. OHG. wart, warto, one who watches, warta a watching, Goth. wardja watchman. See Guard, v. t.]

defs: One who, or that which, guards from injury, danger, exposure, or attack; defense; protection.
quotes: His greatness was no guard to bar heaven's shaft.

defs: A man, or body of men, stationed to protect or control a person or position; a watch; a sentinel.
quotes: The guard which kept the door of the king's house.

defs: One who has charge of a mail coach or a railway train; a conductor.
Any fixture or attachment designed to protect or secure against injury, soiling, or defacement, theft or loss
That part of a sword hilt which protects the hand.
Ornamental lace or hem protecting the edge of a garment.
A chain or cord for fastening a watch to one's person or dress.
A fence or rail to prevent falling from the deck of a vessel.

An extension of the deck of a vessel beyond the hull; esp., in side-wheel steam vessels, the framework of strong timbers, which curves out on each side beyond the paddle wheel, and protects it and the shaft against collision.
A plate of metal, beneath the stock, or the lock frame, of a gun or pistol, having a loop, called a bow, to protect the trigger.
An interleaved strip at the back, as in a scrap book, to guard against its breaking when filled.
A posture of defense in fencing, and in bayonet and saber exercise.
An expression or admission intended to secure against objections or censure.
quotes: They have expressed themselves with as few guards and restrictions as I.

defs: Watch; heed; care; attention; as, to keep guard.
The fibrous sheath which covers the phragmacone of the Belemnites.
notes: ☞ Guard is often used adjectively or in combination; as, guard boat or guardboat; guardroom or guard room; guard duty.
